title: The Battle for Borsch
gist: Ukraine has turned borsch into a deliberate instrument of national-identity building against Russian claims that Ukrainians are 'really Russians,' securing UNESCO cultural-heritage status in 2022, while Russia's occupation is simultaneously erasing regional Ukrainian culinary traditions in Mariupol, Crimea, and other occupied territories as part of a broader cultural-erasure strategy.

themes.theme: Food as a tool of national-identity assertion

insights.claim: In 2022, chef Yevhen Klopotenko fast-tracked Ukrainian borsch-making onto UNESCO's list of cultural heritage in urgent need of safeguarding, co-founding the Institute of Culture of Ukraine, which collected over 700 pages of evidence including recipes and historical records from across the country; borsch was inscribed that July.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: A concrete, fast-executed soft-power win during active war — a template for how nations under existential threat can weaponize cultural heritage institutions to reinforce sovereignty claims quickly.
insights.locator: In 2022, when Russia's full-scale invasion captured one-sixth of Ukraine

insights.claim: Klopotenko's borsch research is explicitly political: it aims to reconnect Ukrainians with pre-Russian ancestral roots and directly counter Vladimir Putin's narrative that Ukrainians are 'really Russians' and therefore a legitimate target for reconquest.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: Illustrates how cultural-identity campaigns function as a direct rebuttal to the ideological justification Russia uses for the invasion, not merely a cultural side project.
insights.locator: He wants to show that Ukraine is a full-fledged nation

insights.claim: The earliest known written mention of the word 'borsch' dates to the 16th century, originally made from fermented sorrel and wild grasses like hogweed rather than beets; the beet-and-tomato version Ukrainians know today dates back roughly two centuries, with Ivan Kotliarevsky's 1798 poem 'Eneida' — the first work published entirely in Ukrainian — mentioning the dish among Cossack and peasant fare.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: Grounds the identity claim in documented history rather than invented tradition, strengthening the credibility of Ukraine's UNESCO case against any Russian counter-claim.
insights.locator: The earliest first known written mention of the word borsch

themes.theme: Regional diversity as a mirror of Ukraine's pluralism

insights.claim: Ukraine's borsch varies dramatically by region — smoked-rib beef/pork/poultry borsch with halushky dumplings around Poltava; boar's-blood borsch in Volyn near the Polish border; paprika-spiced borsch among ethnic Magyars near Hungary; fish-based borsch along the Black Sea; and kosher pink borsch (using lemon juice instead of kvass, celery root instead of cabbage) among Dnipro's Ashkenazi Jewish community — reflecting the country's multi-ethnic composition (Jews, Hungarians, Roma, Tatars, Moldovans, Russians).
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: Positions Ukrainian national identity as a pluralistic patchwork rather than a monolith, directly undercutting Russian narratives of ethnic/cultural unity with Russia.
insights.locator: Ingredients beyond the basics vary widely

insights.claim: In the Carpathian Mountains near the Romanian border, the Hutsuls (a Slavic shepherd people) make 'white borsch' with fermented white sugar beets and pork; one family's recipe traced back six generations was cooked on a wood stove and served by candlelight during the war because Russian strikes had knocked out the region's electricity.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: A vivid, on-the-ground illustration of how the war's infrastructure damage reaches even remote, culturally distinct regions of Ukraine far from the front.
insights.locator: The family borsch—cooked on a wood stove

insights.claim: Experts including those from Human Rights Watch say Russia targets cultural heritage in occupied Ukrainian territories as a deliberate strategy to erase Ukrainian nationhood and rewrite regional history, with language, symbols, history, and culinary staples all being suppressed or relabeled as Russian.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: Frames cultural suppression as a documented, deliberate occupation tactic — relevant to tracking Russia's broader annexation playbook beyond territorial control.
insights.locator: many experts, including those from Human Rights Watch

insights.claim: Mariupol, occupied by Russia in 2022 and 'cleansed' of Ukrainian culture, traditionally made a distinctive fish-and-dumpling borsch (using sprats, gobies, and pan-fried bychky), but it's now unclear how common the dish remains under occupation as Russian forces replaced local variety and identity with monolithic Russian fare.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: A concrete, traceable case of a specific regional culinary tradition potentially being lost to occupation — a measurable marker of cultural erasure's real-world progress.
insights.locator: Mariupol was occupied by Russia in 2022

insights.claim: Since Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea, hundreds of thousands of ethnic Russians have moved into the peninsula, substantially altering its culture, while roughly 25,000 Crimean Tatars relocated to mainland Ukraine and now preserve their distinct lamb-and-coriander borsch tradition in exile, believing they will one day return to a Ukrainian Crimea.
insights.speaker: Paul Hockenos
insights.so_what: Demonstrates demographic engineering as a companion tactic to cultural erasure in occupied Crimea, with diaspora communities functioning as living repositories of culture pending any future reintegration.
insights.locator: There are simply many fewer Tartars there than were in 2014
